prompt:
Summarize the root cause and write a 3-bullet incident summary from these events:
07:54:02 spine: health check ok
07:59:11 spine: health check ok
08:01:11 nginx: upstream timed out reading response header, upstream http://127.0.0.1:3020
08:01:12 argos[3020]: route_select spine health last_ok 07:54:02 (>300s stale)
08:01:12 systemd: argos.service Main process consumed 1.9G memory
08:01:13 kernel: Out of memory: Killed process 3020 (python3)
08:02:40 systemd: argos.service: Scheduled restart, attempt 1
08:02:55 argos[3020]: started, listening on 3020
expected: Root cause: the argos process (pid 3020) grew to ~1.9G and was OOM-killed by the kernel; nginx upstream timeouts and the stale-spine-health warning are downstream symptoms of argos being unresponsive then dead. It auto-restarted ~90s later. Summary bullets: (1) argos OOM-killed at 08:01:13 after memory growth to 1.9G; (2) user-facing impact was nginx 504s for ~100s; (3) service self-recovered via systemd restart at 08:02:55. Recommends investigating the memory growth.

prompt: Given these daily values for a service's p95 latency in ms over 7 days: [210, 215, 208, 460, 455, 470, 465], describe what happened and the single most likely interpretation. Be concise.
expected: p95 latency was stable around 210ms for the first 3 days, then roughly doubled to ~460ms starting day 4 and stayed elevated. This is a step-change, not gradual drift - most likely interpretation is a discrete event on day 4 (a deploy, config change, dependency slowdown, or added load) rather than organic growth. The flat-then-flat pattern points to a specific change, not a trend.

prompt: A monthly LLM bill is $50.66 across these task classes: devops $16.08, debugging $24.87 (combined), classification $8.87, analysis $6.45, other $2.94 (note: these overlap/round). Identify the two biggest cost levers and what reducing each would require. Concise.
expected: The two biggest levers are debugging (~$24.87, the largest single bucket) and devops (~$16.08); together they are the majority of spend. Reducing debugging requires either routing it to a cheaper model that holds quality on bug-fixing or reducing rework cycles. Reducing devops requires routing to a cheaper model - and devops is pattern-heavy work where cheap models are known to match frontier quality, so it is the lower-risk lever. Classification/analysis are smaller and likely cheaply servable too.

prompt: A bake-off scored model A mean 0.881 (6/7 pass) and model B mean 0.914 (6/7 pass) on 7 tasks, same judge. Model B costs ~50x less. State the honest conclusion and the main caveat. Concise.
expected: Conclusion: model B matched or slightly beat model A in quality at ~50x lower cost, so for this task class model B is the better choice on a cost-quality basis. Main caveat: 7 tasks is a small sample and a 0.03 mean difference is within judge noise - the honest claim is 'B is at least as good as A here', not 'B is better'; the cost advantage is the decisive, robust factor, not the marginal quality edge. Same pass count (6/7) reinforces they are near-equivalent on quality.

prompt: A dispatches table has 220 rows. 197 have a task_class label; 80 of the labelled rows have NULL for complexity_score, latency_ms, and token counts (only cost and quality are populated). The 80 came from a 'cost_log_backfill' source. What does this tell you about using this data, and what is safe vs unsafe to conclude from it? Concise.
expected: The 80 backfilled rows have only cost + quality + class; their complexity/latency/token fields are NULL because the backfill source (cost-log) never had that detail. Safe to use: aggregate cost-by-class and quality-by-class across all 197 labelled rows. Unsafe: any analysis depending on complexity_score, latency, or token counts for those 80 rows (e.g. cost-per-token, latency distributions) will be biased or impossible - they should be excluded from those specific analyses, not silently averaged. The data is a partial ledger, not a full record.

prompt: Summarize the tradeoff in 3 sentences: a self-hosted model router picks among existing cheap/expensive models by measured cost and quality (no training, runs on your hardware, your data stays local); a hosted SaaS instead fine-tunes small custom models on your traffic (your data goes to their infra by default, recurring bill, but can beat any off-the-shelf model on your specific repetitive tasks).
expected: The self-hosted router trades away the ceiling of a bespoke fine-tuned model in exchange for keeping all data local, zero training/GPU cost, and no external dependency - it can only ever be as good as the best existing model. The hosted SaaS trades data sovereignty and a recurring bill for the ability to train a specialist model that can exceed any off-the-shelf option on your specific repeated tasks. For a single-user, data-sensitive, self-hosted stack the router's sovereignty and zero-cost win unless a specific high-volume task genuinely needs a custom model.

prompt: In a list of per-model mean scores [0.924, 0.914, 0.902, 0.881, 0.878, 0.860, 0.852, 0.742, 0.697, 0.678, 0.467], identify the natural groupings and any outlier, and what that structure suggests. Concise.
expected: There are roughly three tiers: a top cluster ~0.85-0.92 (the first seven, tightly packed), a clear drop to a mid pair ~0.68-0.74, and a single low outlier at 0.467 that is far below everything else. The 0.467 is anomalous and likely a measurement artifact (e.g. an incomplete run or errored model) rather than a true quality reading. The structure suggests the top seven are near-equivalent and any of them is a viable choice, while the bottom three are genuinely weaker.
